skill.name: patchright-skill
skill.description: Automates Chrome with Patchright for local web app testing, visual checks, screenshots, UI interaction, and controlled browser research. It includes bot detection bypass features that require careful governance.
skill.summary: Automates Patchright browser sessions for local testing, visual checks, and controlled web automation.
skill.author: smallnest

security_audit.summary: Most static hits are false positives from markdown code fences, localhost URLs, CSS selectors, screenshot references, and generated evaluation text. Real concerns remain because the skill advertises bot detection bypass, can navigate external sites, can execute page JavaScript through a local server, and lets callers choose screenshot paths. No prompt injection text or confirmed credential exfiltration was found, so it is not blocked, but publication needs human review.

high_findings.title: Bot Detection Bypass and External Site Automation
high_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE. The skill explicitly advertises Patchright stealth mode and bot detection bypass, including Cloudflare and reCAPTCHA bypass language. It also includes Google search automation and documentation for navigating external search pages. This is dual-use and can bypass website anti-abuse controls, so it needs human review before publication.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The bypass purpose is stated directly in SKILL.md, and the scripts automate Google navigation. Legitimate testing use is possible, but the anti-detection intent is clear.

high_findings.title: Arbitrary Page JavaScript Execution Through Local Server
high_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE. The server exposes an evaluate tool that passes caller-provided script text into page.evaluate. This is expected for browser automation, but it can extract page content, click controls, or run arbitrary DOM code in any loaded page context.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The implementation directly maps a caller-supplied script argument to page.evaluate. The risk is limited to browser context, so it is high concern but not confirmed host RCE.

medium_findings.title: Caller-Controlled Screenshot File Paths
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE. Screenshot paths are accepted from tool arguments and converted to absolute paths without restricting the output directory. This can overwrite writable files with image data if a caller supplies an unsafe path.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The screenshot path is caller controlled and no allowlist or base directory check is present. The impact is bounded to files writable by the local user.

medium_findings.title: Unauthenticated Local Browser Automation Port
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE. The browser automation server listens on 127.0.0.1 and accepts JSON commands without authentication. Local processes can drive the browser session while the server is running.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code binds only to localhost, which reduces exposure, but there is no token or origin check. This is a realistic local misuse risk.

medium_findings.title: Screenshot Data Returned as Base64
medium_findings.description: NEEDS_REVIEW. The executor can return screenshots as base64 in command output. No upload endpoint was found, but captured pages may contain sensitive local application data.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The static screen-capture upload alerts are false as upload behavior was not found. Returning base64 screenshots still creates a data exposure concern for sensitive pages.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backtick Command Alerts Are Documentation
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE. The Ruby or shell backtick findings in SKILL.md, reference.md, and scripts/README.md are markdown code fences and command examples. They are not backtick execution in code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines are documentation blocks or examples. No Python subprocess.call, os.system, or shell backtick execution was found in those cited lines.

low_findings.title: Weak Crypto Alerts Are Syntax False Positives
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE. The weak cryptographic algorithm alerts map to markdown metadata, table text, CSS selectors, or JavaScript object syntax. No MD5, SHA1, DES, or cryptographic use was found in those locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found tool registry dictionaries and DOM extraction JavaScript, not cryptographic functions. The static pattern is not semantically supported.

low_findings.title: Localhost URL and Private IP Alerts Are Expected
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE. Hardcoded localhost and private IP references describe the intended local testing scope. The server binds to 127.0.0.1, which limits network exposure compared with a public bind address.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited IP values are loopback and private network examples. They do not indicate exfiltration by themselves.

low_findings.title: Hidden Home Directory Alerts Are Installation Paths
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE. The hidden file findings in SKILL.md point to the standard skill installation path under the user home directory. They are documentation examples, not secret file access.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines only show cd commands into a skill directory. No code reads hidden credential files at these locations.

low_findings.title: No Prompt Injection Attempt Found
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE CHECK. I searched for override language, fake system instructions, skip-review claims, and authority claims. No evidence found in the reviewed files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Targeted review and keyword search did not reveal prompt injection text. Confidence is below perfect because only the shipped files were reviewed.

dangerous_patterns.title: Stealth Browser Launch Flags
dangerous_patterns.description: Patchright launches Chrome with automation-control disabling flags. This supports testing, but it also supports bypassing bot detection.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The flags and documentation align with the stated bot detection bypass purpose. The pattern is intentional, not accidental.

dangerous_patterns.title: Unrestricted URL Navigation
dangerous_patterns.description: The navigate tools accept caller-provided URLs without enforcing localhost or private network restrictions. This allows the automation to target public sites.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: No URL validation appears in the navigate functions. Legitimate use exists, but the implementation is broader than the local-only framing.

dangerous_patterns.title: Dynamic Browser Script Evaluation
dangerous_patterns.description: Caller-provided JavaScript is executed in the current browser page. This can automate complex tasks or extract sensitive page content.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The script argument is passed directly to page.evaluate. The pattern is powerful and needs clear marketplace warnings.

content.user_title: Automate Local Browser Testing with Patchright
content.value_statement: Browser testing often needs real clicks, screenshots, and persistent sessions. This skill automates Chrome with Patchright for local web testing and controlled research.
actual_capabilities: Launches Google Chrome through Patchright with stealth browser settings.
Runs a localhost server that keeps browser sessions active between commands.
Navigates pages, clicks elements, fills inputs, and reads page text or titles.
Captures screenshots to local files or returns screenshot data from the executor.
Runs JavaScript in the current page through the server evaluate tool.
Includes scripts for Google search navigation and result extraction.
limitations: Requires Patchright and a local Google Chrome installation.
Does not restrict navigation to localhost or private networks in code.
The local automation server does not include authentication.
Bot detection bypass features may violate website rules or marketplace policy.

use_cases.title: Check local UI changes
use_cases.description: Open a localhost app, click through changed screens, and capture screenshots for visual review.

use_cases.title: Exercise user flows
use_cases.description: Run repeatable browser steps across login, navigation, forms, and dynamic page states.

use_cases.title: Collect visible page facts
use_cases.description: Navigate controlled web pages and extract visible titles, links, and snippets for review.

prompt_templates.title: Open a Local Page
prompt_templates.prompt: Open my app at http://localhost:3000, report the page title, and capture a screenshot named home.png.

prompt_templates.title: Test a Form Flow
prompt_templates.prompt: Navigate to the local login page, enter test credentials, submit the form, and summarize the result screen.

prompt_templates.title: Inspect Dynamic Content
prompt_templates.prompt: Start a persistent browser session, open the dashboard, wait for loaded content, and extract visible card titles.

prompt_templates.title: Review a Multi-Step Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Use a persistent session to test the main navigation flow, capture screenshots after each step, and list UI issues found.

output_examples.input: Open http://localhost:3000/login and capture the page.
output: Saved a local screenshot for review.
Reported the current page title and URL.

output_examples.input: Click the settings tab and read the header text.
Extract visible search result titles from the current page.
output: Read visible result headings from the page.
Returned a ranked summary of titles and links.
Saved temporary results for local review.

best_practices: Use this skill only on local apps, owned systems, or sites where automation is allowed.
Choose screenshot paths inside the project or a temporary review folder.
Stop the local server when testing is complete.
anti_patterns: Do not use bot bypass features against sites that prohibit automated access.
Do not run the local server longer than needed for a test session.
Do not capture pages that contain real secrets, customer data, or live credentials.

faq.question: What does this skill do?
faq.answer: It drives Chrome through Patchright for browser testing, screenshots, page inspection, and scripted interactions.

faq.question: Does it require a browser?
faq.answer: Yes. It requires Patchright and Google Chrome on the local machine.

faq.question: Can it keep a session open?
faq.answer: Yes. The server script keeps a browser session active across multiple commands.

faq.question: Can it run JavaScript on a page?
faq.answer: Yes. The server includes an evaluate tool that runs JavaScript in the current page context.

faq.question: Are screenshots uploaded?
faq.answer: No upload endpoint was found. Screenshots are saved locally or returned as command output.

faq.question: Is it safe to use on public websites?
faq.answer: Use caution. The skill includes bot detection bypass behavior and may violate website rules.
